pipeline.cta_desc: Review the information and proceed to the next step.

modal.title: ⚙️ Project configuration
modal.instructions: This data will be used in the PDF report. If a field is left empty, "-" will be written.

file.full_extent_hint: Point cloud perimeter will be used

log.folders_ready: Folders: CLOUDS / RASTER / VECTORS / REPORT — ✅ ready

error.shp_cannot_read: ❌ Could not read the SHP area of interest.
error.shp_multiple_polygons: ❌ The SHP contains %d polygons. The area of interest must have a single polygon. Please select a different file.
error.folder_not_exists: ❌ The results folder does not exist yet.

crs.las_body_1: The LAS/LAZ file has no coordinate reference system (CRS) assigned. To continue, enter the EPSG code corresponding to the LAS data.
crs.las_body_shp_epsg: The loaded SHP uses %s. If both files share the same projection, use that same code.
crs.epsg_reference_suffix: to identify the EPSG code for your reference system.
crs.shp_body_1: The SHP file has no coordinate reference system (CRS) assigned. To continue, enter the EPSG code corresponding to the area of interest.
crs.shp_body_las_epsg: The LAS point cloud uses %s. If both files share the same projection, use that same code.
crs.mismatch_body_1: The point cloud and the area of interest have different projections:
crs.mismatch_body_2: The area of interest will be automatically reprojected to the cloud CRS (%s) before clipping.

subsample.desc: Choose how many points per m² will be loaded. Higher density = higher fidelity and more processing time.
subsample.method_tip: Subsampling types: Random preserves the original distribution. Uniform achieves a homogeneous distribution.

csf.desc: Select the predominant terrain type. The CSF algorithm models a mesh that adapts to the terrain to separate ground from vegetation.

tips.cloud_3d: 🖱️ Rotate: click+drag · Zoom: scroll wheel · Pan: right-click

tips.dem: Zoom with scroll or selection. 🏠 button to reset.
tips.contours: Contour lines plotted over the DEM
tips.hillshade: Relief shading based on solar angle.
tips.profile: Vertical distribution of heights in the point cloud

log.cropping: ✂️  Clipping models to area of interest...

detection.desc: The algorithm searches for the highest point in moving windows. Each maximum represents a tree.

modal.title: Advanced settings — Trees
modal.window_tip: Search window diameter, similar to tree crown size
modal.min_height_tip: Avoids searching for very short trees. Reduces false positives and speeds up the search
modal.canopy_cutoff_tip: Threshold to classify canopy-covered areas. CHM areas above this value are considered covered

tips.apex: Red crosses = apices on CHM. Zoom with scroll or selection.
tips.canopy: Analysis of canopy-covered area using CHM and the configured height threshold.

about.tool_header: ℹ️ About the INTA Tool for comprehensive aerophotogrammetric and LiDAR survey analysis in productive forest landscapes
about.tool_description: Developed by the Forest Group of the INTA Montecarlo Experimental Agricultural Station, this tool presents a workflow for analyzing point clouds generated by aerophotogrammetric or LiDAR surveys.

csf.modal_desc: Manual adjustment of CSF algorithm parameters.
csf.cloth_res_tip: Cell size of the mesh that models the terrain. Smaller values adapt better to micro-relief but require more computation. Very low values may overestimate terrain level under dense vegetation.
csf.threshold_tip: Maximum distance between a point and the mesh to classify it as ground.
csf.slope_tip: Improves mesh coupling in areas with abrupt level changes (road edges, ravines, streams).

dem.res_tip: Pixel size of the elevation model. Lower values = more detail and more computational cost
dem.contour_tip: Vertical spacing between contour lines. Smaller values generate more contours
dem.min_filter_tip: Removes high points from low vegetation. Higher values filter more vegetation (odd, 3–15)
dem.mean_filter_tip: Smooths DEM relief. Higher values produce smoother surfaces (odd, 3–81)

chm.res_tip: Pixel size of the canopy height model. Lower values = more detail (0.1–2 m)
chm.gap_tip: Search radius for CHM gap interpolation. Higher value = more filling (0–0.5 m)

log.shp_no_crs: ⚠️  SHP without projection. Assigning CRS from LAS to area of interest...
log.crs_mismatch: ⚠️  Different CRS. Reprojecting area of interest to LAS CRS...
log.no_points_in_roi: The area of interest contains no points from the cloud. Verify that the SHP polygon overlaps with the LAZ file.
log.dem_stats: Elevation min: %.1f m | max: %.1f m | mean: %.1f m
log.chm_stats: Height min: %.1f m | max: %.1f m | mean: %.1f m
log.detecting_trees: 🌲 Detecting trees — LMF (ws=%.1f m, hmin=%.1f m)...

log.crs_las_assigned: 📐 CRS assigned by user to LAS: EPSG:%d
log.crs_shp_assigned: 📐 CRS assigned by user to SHP: EPSG:%d
log.reprojection: 📐 Reprojection confirmed: SHP EPSG:%s → LAS EPSG:%s.

footer.main: INTA ForestMap — Comprehensive aerial survey analysis — Dr. Eduardo Hildt — INTA EEA Montecarlo —

fig.dem: Digital Elevation Model (DEM) with contour lines

content.area: The analyzed area covers approximately **%s hectares**, delimited by the provided area of interest shapefile.
content.lidr_note: The aerial survey was fully processed using the **lidR** package for R [@lidR2021; @lidR2023], developed specifically for the analysis of airborne LiDAR data in forestry applications and applicable to point clouds obtained by photogrammetry.
content.pointcloud_density: The original point cloud had an estimated density of approximately **%s pts/m²**, with a total of **%s points** in the clipped area with buffer.
content.pointcloud_subsample: To optimize processing time, the cloud was subsampled to a working density of **%s pts/m²**, resulting in **%s points** after filtering.
content.pointcloud_noise: Additionally, duplicate points were removed and noise points were identified and removed using the **SOR** (Statistical Outlier Removal) algorithm.
content.soil_csf: Ground point classification was performed using the **CSF** (Cloth Simulation Filter, Zhang et al. 2016) algorithm, configured for a rigidity level of **%s**, a classification threshold of **%s m**, and a cloth resolution of **%s m**.
content.soil_result: As a result, **%s points** were identified as ground and used to construct the digital elevation model.
content.dem_generation: From the ground-classified points, a Digital Elevation Model (DEM) was generated with a spatial resolution of **%s m**, using triangulated irregular network (TIN) interpolation.
content.dem_smoothing: The model was smoothed in two consecutive stages: first with a minimum window of %sx%s pixels to reduce the influence of residual vegetation, and then with a mean window of %sx%s pixels to obtain a continuous surface.
content.dem_stats: The terrain in the analyzed area had a minimum elevation of **%s m**, maximum of **%s m**, and mean of **%s m**, with an altitudinal range of **%s m**.
content.contours: Contour lines were generated from the smoothed DEM with an interval of **%s m**. The contours were clipped to the area of interest boundaries and exported in shapefile format for use in geographic information systems.
content.chm_normalization: The point cloud was normalized to the terrain level by subtracting the smoothed DEM elevation from each point. This process expresses heights in relative terms to the ground (heights above terrain), independently of the underlying topography.
content.chm_generation: From the normalized cloud, a Canopy Height Model (CHM) was generated with a resolution of **%s m**.
content.chm_stats: The vegetation height in the analyzed area ranged from **%s m** to **%s m**, with a mean height of **%s m**.
content.trees_lmf: Individual tree detection was performed using the **LMF** (Local Maximum Filter) algorithm, which identifies local maxima of the CHM as tree apices.
content.trees_params: A search window of **%s m** diameter and a minimum height of **%s m** were used to exclude shrubs and regeneration.
content.trees_count: A total of **%s trees** were detected in the analyzed area.
content.trees_heights: Estimated individual heights ranged from **%s m** to **%s m**, with a mean height of **%s m**.
content.density_sentence: The density of detected individuals is **%s trees/ha**, with an estimated mean spacing of **%s m** between trees.
content.cv_sentence: The coefficient of variation of heights is **%s%%**, indicating a **%s** stand in terms of vertical structure (Avery and Burkhart, 2002).
content.cv_note: This index is useful for evaluating growth homogeneity and planning differentiated silvicultural interventions.
content.canopy_analysis: The canopy coverage analysis, calculated by vectorizing the binary CHM mask (height threshold = %s m), indicates that the crowns cover **%s%%** of the total area, equivalent to **%s ha**.
content.canopy_importance: This metric is fundamental for evaluating canopy closure, competition between individuals, and space-use efficiency. It is also key for monitoring silvopastoral systems, where canopy coverage determines when pruning and thinning should be performed.
content.products_intro: All products were exported to the selected output folder with the following structure:
content.footer: *Report automatically generated with the INTA Tool for LiDAR Analysis and Forest Photogrammetry (ForestMap - INTA EEA Montecarlo). Statistical values correspond to the processing performed on the indicated date.*

products.classified: - `puntos_clasificados.laz`: Point cloud with ground (Class 2) and vegetation classification.
products.normalized: - `puntos_normalizados.laz`: Point cloud normalized to terrain level.
products.dem: - `DEM.tif`: Smoothed Digital Elevation Model, clipped to the area of interest.
products.chm: - `CHM.tif`: Canopy Height Model, clipped to the area of interest.
products.trees_shp: - `Arboles.shp`: Detected tree points with tree top height.
